meta.title: Portfolio — codeberg
meta.description: Four projects, told properly — the problem, the decisions, and where each landed — plus photographs from away from the screen.

title:
work, told
properly
intro: A grid of thumbnails tells you nothing about how a thing got made. So instead: four projects, each with the problem that started it, the decisions that shaped it, and where it landed — followed by the photographs I make when I put the laptop away.

heroImage.placeholder: Drop the Peakfolio hero — a full product screen works best

paragraphs.label: The problem
paragraphs.text: Developers kept telling me the same thing: they had years of work and nowhere to put it that didn't feel like a template. Every portfolio builder optimized for launch speed, and every result looked identical.

paragraphs.label: What I built
paragraphs.text: A platform where the theming engine is the product: design tokens all the way down, MDX essays as first-class citizens, and a procedural cover system so no two sites ship looking the same. I led design and built the front end over eight months.

aside.label: Where it landed
aside.text: Public beta in spring 2025; 4,000 sites live by year end.

chapters.title: Firelight CMS

paragraphs.text: Editorial teams don't think in database fields — they think in drafts, edits, and deadlines. Firelight started as a weekend prototype for a newsroom friend who was fighting her CMS more than her copy.

That line became the spec. The editor reads like a manuscript, versioning works like a track-changes conversation, and the structured content lives underneath, invisible until a developer needs it. I designed the whole surface and half the API.

chapters.quote: "The best CMS is the one the writer forgets they're using."

inlineImage.placeholder: Drop a detail shot — the editor UI

tallImage.placeholder: Drop a tall Firelight image — mobile view or poster crop

paragraphs.text: Gridly is the project that wasn't supposed to be one. I extracted the constraint-based layout engine from Peakfolio, wrote a README at 2am, and woke up to strangers filing issues. Two years later it's the piece of my work most people have touched without knowing my name — which is exactly what good infrastructure should be.
Maintaining it taught me more about API design than any product role: every abstraction you expose is a promise you keep for years.

sideImage.placeholder: Drop a Gridly image — terminal or docs screenshot

pairImages.placeholder: Drop a field photo — the trails behind the data

paragraphs.text: Ridgeline is where the two halves of this page meet: a dashboard for trail and elevation analytics that started because I wanted better maps of the routes I was photographing. The charts had to feel like the terrain — so elevation profiles render as layered ridgeline plots, and the palette comes straight from fog, stone, and late light.

aside.label: Where it landed
aside.text: Acquired by a mapping company in 2024; the ridgeline chart lives on in their app.

interlude: Everything above was made sitting down.
Everything below is why I stand up.

photography.title: Away from the screen
photography.intro: Mostly mountains, mostly early, shot on whatever's in my pocket. Photography is where I go to stop optimizing — no grid system, no tokens, just weather and patience. These rotate as I shoot more.

in mind?
cta.text: I take on a small number of design and build engagements each year. Tell me what you're making.
